title: Your First Real API
subtitle: Call JSONPlaceholder — a free public API — from your real terminal

sections.content:
You've been calling a mock API. Now let's call a **real** one.

**JSONPlaceholder** (`jsonplaceholder.typicode.com`) is a free public fake API used by millions of developers for testing and learning. No sign-up, no API key, no credit card.

It has real endpoints, real JSON responses, and behaves exactly like a production API — perfect for practicing.
keyPoints: JSONPlaceholder is free, public, and requires no authentication
It has endpoints for users, posts, todos, comments, albums, and photos
Responses are fake data but the format is identical to real APIs
Open your terminal on your real machine for this lesson

sections.instruction: Open your real terminal (not the sandbox). Make sure curl is available.
sections.expectedOutput: curl 7.x.x or higher — any version from the last 5 years will work

troubleshooting.solution: On Mac: curl comes pre-installed. Try restarting your terminal. On Windows: curl is available in PowerShell and Windows Terminal on Windows 10+.

sections.instruction: Fetch a single todo item. This is a real GET request to a real server.

troubleshooting.solution: Check your internet connection. The JSONPlaceholder servers are very reliable — if it doesn't respond, it's likely a local network issue.

sections.instruction: Fetch all users. This returns an array of 10 user objects — scan through the JSON and notice the structure.
sections.expectedOutput: An array of 10 user objects, each with id, name, username, email, address, phone, website, and company fields.

sections.instruction: Use a query parameter to filter: get only the posts by user ID 1.
sections.expectedOutput: An array of 10 post objects, all with userId: 1

sections.instruction: POST a new resource. JSONPlaceholder accepts POST requests and returns the 'created' resource with an ID (though nothing is actually stored).

codeBlocks.code:
curl -X POST https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"title": "My First Post", "body": "Hello from curl!", "userId": 1}'

completionMessage: You just called a real server on the actual internet — no browser, no framework. You fetched data, filtered with query params, and created a resource. This is backend development at its most fundamental.
